title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary: The reviewer raises four issues with the container image build workflow: (1) the base image push will fail with a 403 for fork PRs since `GITHUB_TOKEN` is read-only in that context, and suggests either documenting this limitation, falling back to local builds, or using a service account token; (2) the standalone version tag (e.g., `keystone:28.0.0`) is mutable and silently overwritten by builds from different branches, and should be branch-qualified or restricted to a single canonical branch; (3) the declared `image-ref` job output is unused, unreliable with matrix expansion, and should be removed or properly wired up via `workflow_call`; (4) base images are tagged only as `:latest` with no immutable history, and should additionally be tagged with `${{ github.sha }}` for auditability and rollback.

code_comments.body:
Base image push will fail for fork PRs

The `build-base-images` job unconditionally pushes to GHCR on every trigger, including `pull_request` events. For PRs opened from forks, GitHub Actions automatically restricts `GITHUB_TOKEN` to read-only, regardless of the `packages: write` permission declared in the job. The `docker push` step will fail with a 403, breaking CI for any external contributor opening a fork PR.

The design intentionally pushes base images on PRs (because `docker-image://` URIs require registry availability), but this makes the workflow incompatible with fork PRs without a workaround. Options to consider:
- Explicitly document that fork PRs are not supported (and add a workflow condition to fail fast with a clear error message rather than failing mid-build)
- Conditionally skip the `docker-image://` build-context path for fork PRs and fall back to building base images locally with `--load` (though this increases complexity)
- Use a dedicated service account token stored as a repository secret (`GHCR_TOKEN`) instead of `GITHUB_TOKEN` for the registry push, which would work for fork PRs if the secret is accessible

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: .github/workflows/build-images.yaml
Line: 52

Comment:
Base image push will fail for fork PRs

The `build-base-images` job unconditionally pushes to GHCR on every trigger, including `pull_request` events. For PRs opened from forks, GitHub Actions automatically restricts `GITHUB_TOKEN` to read-only, regardless of the `packages: write` permission declared in the job. The `docker push` step will fail with a 403, breaking CI for any external contributor opening a fork PR.

The design intentionally pushes base images on PRs (because `docker-image://` URIs require registry availability), but this makes the workflow incompatible with fork PRs without a workaround. Options to consider:
- Explicitly document that fork PRs are not supported (and add a workflow condition to fail fast with a clear error message rather than failing mid-build)
- Conditionally skip the `docker-image://` build-context path for fork PRs and fall back to building base images locally with `--load` (though this increases complexity)
- Use a dedicated service account token stored as a repository secret (`GHCR_TOKEN`) instead of `GITHUB_TOKEN` for the registry push, which would work for fork PRs if the secret is accessible

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Added a 'Reject fork PRs' conditional step at the top of build-base-images (lines 32-40) that checks github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name != github.repository and exits with a ::error:: message explaining that fork PRs cannot push to GHCR. Documentation updated with a callout block (docs line 33-36) and a new step row in the build-base-images steps table (docs line 108).

code_comments.body:
Mutable `version` tag is silently overwritten across branches

The standalone version tag (e.g., `keystone:28.0.0`) is pushed unconditionally on every push event, from any branch. Since both `main` and `stable/**` branches can build the same upstream version (`28.0.0`), concurrent or sequential builds from different branches will overwrite each other's `keystone:28.0.0` tag — the last writer wins silently.

The composite tag (`28.0.0-p0-main-a1b2c3d`) is unique per build and unambiguous. But the version-only shortcut tag is mutable across branches, which means a deployment system referencing `keystone:28.0.0` could silently receive an image built from a different branch (e.g., `stable/2025.2` instead of `main`) without any indication.

Consider qualifying the version tag with the branch to avoid clobbering, e.g. `keystone:28.0.0-main` and `keystone:28.0.0-stable-2025.2`, or restricting the version tag push to a single canonical branch (e.g., only `main`).

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: .github/workflows/build-images.yaml
Line: 142

Comment:
Mutable `version` tag is silently overwritten across branches

The standalone version tag (e.g., `keystone:28.0.0`) is pushed unconditionally on every push event, from any branch. Since both `main` and `stable/**` branches can build the same upstream version (`28.0.0`), concurrent or sequential builds from different branches will overwrite each other's `keystone:28.0.0` tag — the last writer wins silently.

The composite tag (`28.0.0-p0-main-a1b2c3d`) is unique per build and unambiguous. But the version-only shortcut tag is mutable across branches, which means a deployment system referencing `keystone:28.0.0` could silently receive an image built from a different branch (e.g., `stable/2025.2` instead of `main`) without any indication.

Consider qualifying the version tag with the branch to avoid clobbering, e.g. `keystone:28.0.0-main` and `keystone:28.0.0-stable-2025.2`, or restricting the version tag push to a single canonical branch (e.g., only `main`).

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Restricted the version-only tag (e.g. keystone:28.0.0) to main branch only using a conditional expression: ${{ github.ref_name == 'main' && steps.tags.outputs.version || '' }} (workflow line 180). Added an inline comment (lines 175-177) explaining the rationale. Documentation Tag Schema table updated to show version tag applies to 'main only' (docs lines 213, 216-218).

code_comments.body:
Unused `image-ref` job output may mislead future maintainers

The `image-ref` output is declared on `build-service-images` but — as the comment correctly notes — is not consumed by any job in this workflow. The `smoke-test` job derives its own refs independently. 

While the docs (`docs/reference/build-images-workflow.md` lines 168–172) document this as intentionally available for external `workflow_call` consumers, there are currently no callers and the behavior on matrix expansion (silently capturing only the last entry's value) makes it unreliable for that purpose too.

Consider either removing the output to reduce confusion, or adding a `workflow_call` trigger with an explicit output mapping that documents the multi-entry limitation. Leaving an unused output with a "here be dragons" comment increases maintenance overhead when the matrix grows.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: .github/workflows/build-images.yaml
Line: 85-86

Comment:
Unused `image-ref` job output may mislead future maintainers

The `image-ref` output is declared on `build-service-images` but — as the comment correctly notes — is not consumed by any job in this workflow. The `smoke-test` job derives its own refs independently. 

While the docs (`docs/reference/build-images-workflow.md` lines 168–172) document this as intentionally available for external `workflow_call` consumers, there are currently no callers and the behavior on matrix expansion (silently capturing only the last entry's value) makes it unreliable for that purpose too.

Consider either removing the output to reduce confusion, or adding a `workflow_call` trigger with an explicit output mapping that documents the multi-entry limitation. Leaving an unused output with a "here be dragons" comment increases maintenance overhead when the matrix grows.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Removed the image-ref output declaration from the build-service-images job entirely. The job now declares no outputs. Documentation updated at line 172-173 to explicitly state 'This job does not declare outputs. The smoke-test job derives its own image refs independently via its own matrix strategy (CC-0007).'

code_comments.body:
Base images tagged only as `:latest` with no immutable history

Both `python-base` and `venv-builder` are pushed with a single `:latest` tag. Each workflow run overwrites the previous tag, including PR builds (where `GITHUB_TOKEN` has the rights). While the `build-service-images` job pins to the exact digest produced in the same run — preserving per-run reproducibility — there is no way to identify which commit produced the currently-published `:latest`, or to retrieve an earlier base image version without the digest from a workflow run artifact.

Adding a SHA-tagged label (e.g., `python-base:${{ github.sha }}`) alongside `:latest` would provide an immutable reference for auditing and rollback. Even a simple `${{ github.sha }}` tag would make it trivial to correlate a base image in GHCR to the commit that produced it.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: .github/workflows/build-images.yaml
Line: 53

Comment:

Both `python-base` and `venv-builder` are pushed with a single `:latest` tag. Each workflow run overwrites the previous tag, including PR builds (where `GITHUB_TOKEN` has the rights). While the `build-service-images` job pins to the exact digest produced in the same run — preserving per-run reproducibility — there is no way to identify which commit produced the currently-published `:latest`, or to retrieve an earlier base image version without the digest from a workflow run artifact.

Adding a SHA-tagged label (e.g., `python-base:${{ github.sha }}`) alongside `:latest` would provide an immutable reference for auditing and rollback. Even a simple `${{ github.sha }}` tag would make it trivial to correlate a base image in GHCR to the commit that produced it.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Added a :${{ github.sha }} tag alongside :latest for both python-base (workflow line 65) and venv-builder (workflow line 80). Documentation updated at lines 112-113 to list both tags in the steps table, and lines 119-121 explain the dual-tagging rationale with the SHA tag providing an auditable commit-to-image mapping.

resolution_summary: All four review comments were addressed in .github/workflows/build-images.yaml and docs/reference/build-images-workflow.md. Fork PRs are now rejected early with a clear error message, the mutable version tag is restricted to main-only pushes, the unused image-ref job output was removed from build-service-images, and base images now receive an immutable :${{ github.sha }} tag alongside :latest. Documentation was updated to reflect all four changes.
